data.epi_content:
When did I realize the cookie jar I'd received was almost empty?

Perhaps it was when I started to notice a faint cocoa scent rising even as I breathed.

If I had to make an excuse, I was quite hungry.

Enough to eat rather urgently with just a glass of water, though not exactly wolfing it down.

"We missed each other. I just didn't like it."

Why couldn't I speak up in the first place?

Rather than reaching philosophical ponderings about what kind of being I am, I simply recall the emotions I felt then.

So many unnecessary feelings—stubborn pride, jealousy, misery, inferiority—mixed together and made my lips feel incredibly heavy.

As if they might tear and fall off if I opened them even slightly.

The cookies were actually delicious.

But I wanted to say they weren't.

Come to think of it, the circumstances don't quite add up.

Parents dead, fortune dissolved into thin air, no one around to help me—I became an ill-mannered little lady simply because I was reading a romance novel.

Thanks to that, if I leave this room and wander around, I'll be pointed at, whispered about, and mocked.

It would be nice if it stopped there, but it usually goes further.

I wish I had some advantage, but there probably isn't one. If there were, it would have been placed somewhere visible from the start.

Unless I was some self-assured person with special abilities.

Last time I wanted oolong tea, but this time I want to listen to music.

Music I could easily access with just a few finger movements.

It would be nice if there were a piano in the room.

In a world patched together with all sorts of convenient settings, having a piano in the room might not be too much to ask.

Light gradually began to disappear from the room.

As if there's no need to shine light where the protagonist isn't present.

In reality, day was ending and night was arriving.

Rather than focusing on how the moon pulled down the sun and climbed above it, I find it more relatable to lament my unfortunate circumstances.

Night was dark, making it a perfect environment for gloomy and cringeworthy thoughts.

And there was one very simple solution.

To think sleepy thoughts and drift off to sleep.

I trudged to the bed and collapsed onto it.

Even moving this dreadful body felt terribly heavy.

Tomorrow probably won't bring any improvements, but still, hoping for a better tomorrow... perhaps "hoping" is too grand a word.

I simply lay in bed wishing for a better tomorrow and fell asleep.

Not a chance.

.

.

.

.

.

The morning was quite bright.

Only because I forgot to close the curtains, letting sunlight flood the room.

"Ugh."

As I tried to get up by putting my foot down, it got cut by a piece of glass I hadn't managed to clean up.

The breaking sound had been faint, but glass is glass—it must have scattered when it shattered.

It seems no one has come by today.
data.epi_content2:
Ah, come to think of it, I haven't left the room.

Nor have I collapsed.

The Academy dormitory is like staying at a hotel—occasionally someone comes to clean the room, replenish towels, and fill up the basic tea leaves provided (usually cheap ones that commoners can easily afford).

Vivian must also stay in the dormitory. I wonder why she cleaned up herself instead of letting the cleaners do it.

She probably just cleaned it because it was visible and seemed dangerous.

Just the large pieces that were visible.

Even though it ended up being useless, I didn't even thank her.

Was I unable to?

I don't know if I'm not asking for help or if I can't, but that's the kind of person I am.

The glass shard is so deeply embedded in my skin that I can't easily remove it with my fingers.

Unable to put my foot on the ground, I limp to the drawer, take out a small knife, quickly wash it with hot water, and press it against my foot.

Tsk.

I apply pressure to cut the flesh, and the glass shard pops out.

It gives me a slightly chilling feeling, but I need to remove it right away before it gets infected.

It hurts.

Blood, looking more black than red, begins to drip onto the floor.

This too could be quickly cleaned with magic.

I place a white cloth under my foot and put on rayon stockings.

It looks a bit unnatural, but it won't matter since it will be covered by shoes.

Enjoying the soft feeling against my skin, I gather my bag and walk to the entrance to leave the room.

And I open the door.

Why is it so hard to take a step when it's just one step?

Still, I gather my courage and step forward.

I see a long corridor.

I see students passing by.

They must be whispering about me.

"How pitiful," "She had bad luck, serves her right," "I don't like how she still holds her head high," "I don't understand why she doesn't leave the Academy."

They must be saying such things.

I can't hear them, but surely.

But I move forward toward the classroom, thinking I won't pay any attention to it.

The classes I attend are mostly about common sense or how to use magic.

There's no need to take exams or actively participate.

Commoners have monthly tests and get expelled if they fall below standards, but I just need to sit in my seat and maintain attendance.

Thanks to that, in the novel, characters could skip classes they were supposed to attend and travel around, advancing the story.

The Academy is just a place where children of nobles from the provinces are gathered—essentially held hostage, to put it bluntly.

Fortunately, I didn't encounter anyone on my way to the classroom.

And unfortunately, as soon as I entered the classroom, I came face to face with someone I didn't want to meet.

"Oh my, what brings you here, miss?

It's been a while! I was worried since you haven't shown your face for some time."

"...Lydia."

Villainous women in romance novels usually lead followers.

Their job is to flatter their master, match their mood, and move according to their taste without being told directly, making their master happy.
data.epi_content3:
Of course, there must be some benefit and something to satisfy their pride.

If their former master suddenly falls and becomes miserable, these subordinates prefer to trample on their old master and humiliate them rather than sympathize.

This girl was no different.

"You weren't avoiding class because you were upset about that little prank I played, were you?"

Well, I don't think locking someone in a closet for several hours is a little prank.

Especially when the reason was refusing to eat garbage collected from the floor that she offered me.

"Oh, it seems you've given up on being angry.

That's a good sign! If you give up one thing after another, you'll eventually listen to me well.

By the way, I heard an interesting story."

She puts on a slightly wicked smile and assumes a startled posture that is clearly meant to mock.

Then she continues with a taunting tone.

"I heard that Duke Mecklenburg wasn't actually killed in an accident but was found hanging from a rope!

Haha, an accidental death when rumors of treason were swirling around—who would believe that? Don't you agree, miss?"

She's talking about my father.

Of course, as memories pile up in my head, I try to tell myself he's not my father, but I simply can't.

The positive memories—gifts given on birthdays, occasional words of encouragement, the calm atmosphere during family meals—have lingered stickily in my mind, making him my dear father for a long time.

I could choose to get angry at this story and hit this red-headed creature, who can barely be distinguished as human, with whatever I can grab.

But what happens after that is the problem.

Teachers won't care much whether violence occurs here or not.

Or rather, they can't.

Not many people can scold a student who might be of higher status, or who could crush them financially or threaten their livelihood through connections.

If a student asks a teacher to leave the classroom, the teacher has to leave—that says it all.

It's quite contradictory.

They gather nobles in the middle of the capital to control them, but they only gather them without managing them.

They've brought them here, but as long as they don't cross certain lines, they don't care what mischief they get up to.

I hear some children die occasionally, but why should anyone care?

It can all be covered up and resolved.

So if I were to hit the child in front of me, next time several people would approach to harm me.

After politely asking the teacher to take attendance and leave the classroom.

Violence rarely happens—it's usually one-sided bullying—but if I were to hit this child, that's what would happen.

And regardless of all that, I couldn't stop my hands and feet from trembling.

Because I was angry.

Not wanting to show such a state, I pressed my foot, which felt slightly wet with blood, firmly to the floor and folded my hands neatly to hide the trembling.

But I got even angrier at myself for being pathetic, just standing there taking insults without moving.

"It's amazing how calm you remain hearing such things.

The other children you told me to bully would scream and make a fuss whenever their parents were mentioned, hee.

Well, it was nice seeing you after so long."

She smiles, walks to another seat, opens a book, and starts reading.

Other students will start arriving soon, and she won't bully me publicly in front of others.

She was quite careful about her reputation and cautious.

I won't go around telling everyone I'm being bullied either.

I don't know why...

Because this white-haired young lady still has unnecessary pride and stubbornness.

Perhaps it's the last bit of self-respect.
